title: Odeo

infobox.value: was an American directory and search destination website for [RSS](/wiki/RSS "RSS")-syndicated audio and video. It employed tools that enabled users to create, record, and share [podcasts](/wiki/Podcasting "Podcasting") with a simple [Adobe Flash](/wiki/Adobe_Flash "Adobe Flash")-based interface.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American website (2005–2017)

Odeo

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Type of site | Podcast publishing and aggregation |
| Owner | [Obvious Corporation](/wiki/Obvious_Corporation "Obvious Corporation") (2006–2007) Sonic Mountain (2007–2010) |
| Created by | [Evan Williams](/wiki/Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur) "Evan Williams (Internet entrepreneur)") [Noah Glass](/wiki/Noah_Glass_(Twitter) "Noah Glass (Twitter)") |
| URL | [odeo.com](https://web.archive.org/web/20100212194957/http://www.odeo.com/) |
| Registration | Free |
| Launched | July 10, 2005 |
| Current status | domain expired in 2017 |

**Odeo** was an American directory and search destination website for [RSS](/wiki/RSS "RSS")-syndicated audio and video. It employed tools that enabled users to create, record, and share [podcasts](/wiki/Podcasting "Podcasting") with a simple [Adobe Flash](/wiki/Adobe_Flash "Adobe Flash")-based interface.

Odeo was originally developed in 2004[[1]](#cite_note-1) by founders [Noah Glass](/wiki/Noah_Glass_(Twitter) "Noah Glass (Twitter)") and [Evan Williams](/wiki/Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur) "Evan Williams (Internet entrepreneur)"), who were the founders of Audioblog and [Pyra Labs](/wiki/Pyra_Labs "Pyra Labs"), respectively, and received funding from [Charles River Ventures](/wiki/Charles_River_Ventures "Charles River Ventures"). Subsequently, Williams bought out Charles River's interest in the company, as well as that of several other investors, and re‑formed the organization under a new company, [Obvious Corporation](/wiki/Obvious_Corporation "Obvious Corporation"), which planned to develop new products, including [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Odeo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[Kevin Systrom](/wiki/Kevin_Systrom "Kevin Systrom"), cofounder of [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram"), was an intern at Odeo during the summer of 2005.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

On February 19, 2007, Williams wrote in his [blog](/wiki/Blog "Blog") that Odeo was for sale.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It was acquired soon afterwards by New York-based Sonic Mountain.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

On September 14, 2007, Sonic Mountain announced that it had acquired the technology assets of FireAnt, an RSS [video-aggregation](/wiki/Video_aggregator "Video aggregator") website and desktop media player, and that it planned to incorporate FireAnt's technology into Odeo.[[5]](#cite_note-5) On March 18, 2008, Sonic Mountain announced that it had acquired Blogdigger, a [search engine](/wiki/Search_engine "Search engine") for blogs and RSS content, with plans to incorporate the technology into Odeo.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In June 2008, Odeo was relaunched by Sonic Mountain with a completely redesigned site and an expanded focus on search and discovery for syndicated audio and video.

In March 2009, Odeo began its transition to a fee-based service for large corporations looking to manage their video libraries. Targeting companies that needed to organize and track video usage, Odeo released its “Enterprise Video Management” platform as a [Software as a Service](/wiki/Software_as_a_Service "Software as a Service") (SaaS) solution. Their first customers included such notables as [American Express](/wiki/American_Express "American Express") and eGA.

In August 2010, Odeo suspended its support of the consumer site odeo.com to focus its attention on enterprise video.

As of 2017, Odeo's domain has expired and has been sold.
